## 2. Related Work

### 2.1 Regime Detection in Financial Markets

The concept that financial markets operate in distinct states with different statistical properties has a long history in econometrics. Hamilton's (1989) seminal Markov switching model for business cycles established the framework for regime-dependent modelling, demonstrating that economic time series exhibit discrete structural breaks that are better captured by state-dependent parameters than by a single stationary process. The framework was extended to financial volatility by Hamilton and Susmel (1994), who combined Markov switching with autoregressive conditional heteroskedasticity to model regime-dependent volatility clustering. The volatility-regime extension is directly relevant to the present work: as Section 6.3 reports, volatility-family features dominate the mutual-information ranking on EUR-USD 5m data, indicating that the regime structure most useful for grid-hedged Martingale strategies is a volatility-regime structure.

Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) have since become the dominant framework for regime identification in financial applications. Nystrup et al. (2017) used adaptive HMMs with time-varying parameters for dynamic asset allocation, showing that allowing transition probabilities to evolve over time improved portfolio performance compared to static HMM specifications. Their work highlighted the importance of *persistent* states, i.e. regimes stable enough to inform trading decisions before the next transition occurs. Nystrup et al. (2020) further developed this idea by penalising jumps in the HMM estimation, encouraging the discovery of persistent rather than transient states. Palupi et al. (2021) demonstrated the predictive value of HMM-based regime classification across diverse international markets including the Jakarta Stock Exchange, finding that regime-aware portfolio allocation outperformed regime-agnostic benchmarks. Yang et al. (2025) applied an HMM-based regime model in service of an evolutionary alpha-generation framework (discussed in §2.2 and §2.3). Gopinathan et al. (2024) combined Gaussian mixtures with HMMs in a stock-price prediction setting, showing that GMM-HMM hybrids capture conditional distributional structure that single-component HMMs miss. The shared limitation across this lineage is that the regime model is fitted independently of the trading policy: regime estimation optimises a likelihood objective on market features, while parameter adjustment is applied post hoc to the inferred regime label. The decoupling is a structural property of the HMM-then-trade pipeline rather than a defect of any single paper.

Gaussian Mixture Models provide an alternative that does not impose temporal transition structure, instead classifying each observation independently based on feature-space proximity (McLachlan & Peel, 2000). The independence assumption has trade-offs: it cannot capture sequential dependencies between regimes, but it avoids the strong Markov assumption that future states depend only on the current state and not on the duration spent there. Wang and Aste (2023) employed inverse covariance clustering to identify temporal market clusters for dynamic portfolio optimisation, hypothesising that distinct distribution states exist at various intervals and showing that cluster-based portfolio construction outperformed standard mean-variance optimisation. More recently, Issa and Horvath (2023) developed a non-parametric online regime-detection method using maximum mean discrepancy on path-dependent data structures, representing the leading non-parametric alternative to parametric mixture modelling. We adopt the parametric mixture path because our downstream architecture requires a finite, labelled regime set to seed islands; non-parametric path-space methods do not yield the discrete leaf identities our island topology consumes.

Hierarchical structure in market regimes has independent empirical support. Cont (2001) demonstrated that financial return series exhibit nested distributional regularities, providing one of the earliest motivations for hierarchical rather than flat clustering. More recently, Orton and Gebbie (2024) studied representation learning for regime detection in *block-hierarchical* financial markets, providing direct empirical motivation for a two-level macro-cluster + sub-cluster regime decomposition: the macro level captures broad correlation-block structure, and the sub-level captures finer distributional heterogeneity within each block. Our hierarchical GMM choice (Section 3) implements this two-level decomposition through Bayesian Information Criterion selection at each level, building on Ward's (1963) classical agglomerative-hierarchical-clustering objective and the modern revision of Ward's criterion by Murtagh and Legendre (2014).

A methodologically distinct alternative is *change-point detection*, where regime boundaries are inferred as discrete temporal break points rather than continuous mixture-component memberships. Killick et al. (2012) developed the PELT algorithm, which provides optimal change-point detection at linear computational cost and remains the dominant non-mixture approach. We chose probabilistic mixture modelling over change-point segmentation because the downstream island architecture requires per-candle continuous regime classification (with hysteresis-stabilised transitions, Section 3.6), not discrete temporal segments. Change-point methods would also need to be paired with a separate per-segment parameter-selection mechanism, reintroducing the regime-then-policy decoupling that our integrated approach is designed to eliminate.

Across these methodological directions, the common pattern is that regime detection optimises for classification accuracy on market features, which is not the same objective as the downstream trading performance. Ang and Timmermann (2012) survey regime-switching models in financial markets, explicitly distinguishing regime identification (state-label estimation) from regime application (decisions conditioned on those labels) and noting that the two are typically modelled in separate stages. This research addresses that decoupling by making regime structure the *topology* of the evolutionary search itself: the islands of the GA *are* the regime leaves, and parameter optimisation per regime is therefore not an after-the-fact step but the primary loop.

### 2.2 Evolutionary Computation for Trading Systems

Genetic programming was applied to foreign-exchange trading rules in Neely, Weller and Dittmar (1997), who evolved technical rules for major currency pairs and reported positive in-sample profitability that partially survived out-of-sample testing under realistic transaction costs. Genetic algorithms then entered the broader trading-system literature with Allen and Karjalainen (1999), who evolved technical trading rules for the S&P 500 and found that the evolved rules did not consistently outperform a buy-and-hold benchmark after accounting for transaction costs. Their negative finding was an early warning that the search problem is structurally harder than fitness alone suggests: evolutionary computation can locate parameter configurations that look profitable on training data but fail to transfer because the underlying signal does not have the directional edge the GA's fitness function presumed. Aguilar-Rivera et al. (2015) survey genetic algorithms, genetic programming, multi-objective evolutionary algorithms, and estimation-of-distribution algorithms across financial applications, identifying parameter sensitivity and overfitting as persistent challenges.

Dempster and Jones (2001) demonstrated a real-time adaptive trading system using genetic programming to evolve technical trading rules for GBP/USD foreign exchange, applying walk-forward re-optimisation as market conditions changed. Their key innovation was *online re-evolution*: the GP population was periodically re-trained on recent data, allowing evolved rules to adapt to structural changes. This addresses regime sensitivity *implicitly*, through recency-weighted training data, rather than explicitly through regime detection. The contrast with our work is direct: Dempster and Jones treat non-stationarity as a temporal-recency problem, our approach treats it as a regime-structure problem, and the two views imply different architectures (rolling re-evolution of one population vs. per-regime isolated populations evaluated on a fixed but regime-segmented training window).

Aguilar-Rivera and Valenzuela-Rendon (2019) developed a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for multi-period portfolio optimisation incorporating dynamic restrictions such as transaction costs and inflation, moving beyond single-objective fitness functions. Zhang et al. (2020) proposed AutoAlpha for hierarchical search and ensemble ranking of alpha factors, applying evolutionary computation to the problem of discovering predictive signals rather than optimising fixed trading rules. Almeida and Neves (2022) introduced a self-adaptive evolutionary algorithm with hybrid genetic operators for portfolio optimisation, demonstrating that *adaptive operator selection* during search reduces the brittleness of fixed-operator GAs in non-stationary financial environments. Long and Kampouridis (2025) developed a genetic algorithm that evolves the *full multi-threshold parameter space* of directional-changes trading strategies, including entry, exit, and exposure thresholds simultaneously, confirming the contemporary trend toward complete-genome evolution rather than restricted parameter subsets. Yang et al. (2025) introduced a co-evolutionary genetic programming framework in which subpopulations evolve independently per market regime, each generating regime-specific alpha factor expressions combined through an HMM-based gating mechanism. Their work is the closest prior work to this research, sharing the principle of per-regime evolutionary isolation while differing in the representation (GP expressions vs. fixed-parameter vectors), the regime discovery framework (HMM-based vs. hierarchical GMM), and the application domain (alpha factor construction vs. strategy execution parameters).

The theoretical foundation for per-regime subpopulation evolution lies in the niching and fitness-sharing literature (Goldberg & Richardson, 1987; Mahfoud, 1995). Mahfoud's (1995) thesis is the canonical treatment of niching methods for genetic algorithms, formalising the conditions under which subdivided populations preserve diversity across distinct fitness peaks rather than collapsing to a single global optimum. Our island-per-regime approach is a discrete, regime-conditioned form of niching where the subpopulation boundaries are not emergent (as in fitness-sharing or crowding) but pre-specified by the regime tree. The regime tree thus serves the same role that a niche-radius parameter plays in fitness-sharing: it defines the partition of the fitness landscape that each subpopulation is permitted to specialise in.

Reinforcement learning has emerged as the dominant alternative paradigm for adaptive trading systems. Liu et al. (2020) introduced FinRL as a deep RL framework for automated stock trading, and a body of recent work extends RL specifically to regime-aware policies. Bauman et al. (2024) embedded regime-probability estimates into the state representation of a deep RL agent for goal-based investing under regime switching, reporting gains over non-regime-conditioned baselines. The architectural distinction between RL and our evolutionary approach is structural: deep RL learns a single black-box policy network that maps state (including regime) to action, while our island-model GA maintains a population of explicit interpretable parameter configurations per regime. The RL approach has higher representational capacity but is more vulnerable to non-stationarity and Q-function instability under leveraged grid-Martingale dynamics; the EC approach trades capacity for interpretability and per-regime isolation. The two paradigms address related problems with structurally different solutions.

Overfitting remains a persistent challenge regardless of paradigm: evolved or learned solutions may exploit historical noise rather than genuine market structure (Bailey et al., 2014; McLean & Pontiff, 2016). Bailey et al. (2014) demonstrated that the probability of backtest overfitting (PBO) increases rapidly with the number of trials, reaching near-certainty for typical optimisation runs. The methodological framework has since been substantially expanded: Lopez de Prado (2018) develops walk-forward purging and embargo techniques to suppress information leakage in financial cross-validation, and Arian et al. (2024) compare walk-forward, K-fold, purged K-fold, and combinatorial purged cross-validation (CPCV) on synthetic regime-switching data, quantifying the residual PBO under each. We address overfitting through two mechanisms. First, per-regime evolution restricts each population to samples from a single market state, reducing the diversity of conditions each genome must accommodate and thereby reducing the incentive to overfit to transitional patterns. Second, strict hold-out validation: training uses data through December 2024, evaluation begins January 2025. We acknowledge that hold-out is a weaker protocol than CPCV and is sensitive to the specific OOS window; a walk-forward validation protocol with rolling re-training is in progress for an extended conference submission, and CPCV adaptation to per-regime island populations is identified as future work.

### 2.3 Island-Model Genetic Algorithms

The island model, also known as the multi-deme model, maintains multiple semi-isolated populations that evolve independently with periodic migration of individuals between islands (Whitley et al., 1999). The structure preserves diversity by allowing different populations to explore different regions of the search space while migration prevents premature convergence (Alba & Tomassini, 2002). The biological analogy draws on Wright's (1931) shifting balance theory from population genetics, where subdivided populations explore fitness landscapes more effectively than a single large population. The foundational works establish the diversity-preservation rationale for islands but do not specify how the topology should be derived for problem-specific applications: the islands of the standard model are interchangeable subpopulations, and the migration graph is typically chosen heuristically.

The migration topology (ring, star, random, or problem-specific) determines how genetic material flows between populations and affects convergence behaviour (Cantu-Paz, 2000). Ring topologies restrict gene flow to nearest neighbours, maintaining higher inter-population diversity at the cost of slower global convergence. Star topologies centralise gene flow through a hub population, accelerating convergence but potentially reducing diversity. Random topologies provide intermediate behaviour. The choice of topology, migration rate, and migration interval collectively define the island model's exploration-exploitation trade-off, but the literature lacks principled criteria for *deriving* a topology from the problem domain rather than imposing one heuristically.

Lopes et al. (2012) proposed a multi-agent approach to dynamically adapt migration topology using Q-learning, demonstrating competitive performance against static ring and random topologies on benchmark optimisation functions. Their adaptive approach allowed the topology to evolve during the optimisation process, responding to population dynamics rather than remaining fixed. Chideme, Chen, and Lin (2025) introduced three parallel island-model architectures (MSGTSP, IGTSP-Ring, IGTSP-Multikuti) for Group Trading Strategy Portfolio Optimization, achieving computational speed-up ratios of 157% to 287% over sequential methods, with IGTSP-Ring exhibiting the lowest coefficient of variation in high-volatility environments. However, in both Lopes et al. (2012) and Chideme et al. (2025), the islands serve as parallel search populations on a single shared problem rather than as representations of distinct market states; the topology is adapted or chosen but not *grounded* in problem structure.

The theoretical bridge between the island model and our regime-conditioned approach lies in the niching and fitness-sharing literature (Goldberg & Richardson, 1987; Mahfoud, 1995). Niching methods preserve population diversity across distinct peaks of a multimodal fitness landscape; the island model is the spatial analogue, where subpopulation boundaries are explicit rather than implicit. Mahfoud's (1995) treatment formalises the conditions under which niching converges to multiple peaks rather than collapsing to a single global optimum. The conditions are sharpest when the subpopulation boundaries align with structural partitions of the fitness landscape, which is exactly the regime structure our hierarchical GMM discovers. The connection is rarely made explicit in the EC-for-trading literature: islands are typically motivated by parallelism and diversity rather than by a niching theorem applied to a known multimodal landscape.

To the best of our knowledge, this research is the first to use market regimes as the structuring principle for island topology in a trading strategy *execution-parameter* optimisation system evaluated under a realistic execution engine. Each island corresponds to a discovered market state, and sibling migration occurs between sub-regimes of the same macro-cluster. Yang et al. (2025) share the concept of per-regime subpopulations but evolve genetic programming expressions for alpha factors via co-evolutionary feedback, whereas this work evolves fixed-length parameter vectors via an island-model GA with ring-topology sibling migration, applied to trading strategy execution parameters rather than factor construction, and evaluated on a production backtester with real transaction costs.

### 2.4 Grid-Hedged Martingale Strategies and Risk Management

The Martingale betting strategy, originating in 18th-century probability theory, doubles the stake after each loss to recover all prior losses upon the first win (Doob, 1953). In financial markets, Martingale-family strategies have been adapted as grid trading systems that open additional positions at fixed price intervals against an adverse move, with take-profit targets designed to recover the aggregate position's cost basis (Carricaburu, c. 2004). The retail and algorithmic forex literature has continued to refine this pattern; Rundo et al. (2019) developed the GTSBot grid trading system as one of the few peer-reviewed academic treatments of grid execution on FX, providing a baseline against which the present work compares (Section 5).

The fundamental mathematical property of Martingale-family strategies is that they trade a high probability of small wins for a small probability of catastrophic loss. For a grid-hedged variant with maximum depth *N*, geometric multiplier *m*, and per-level loss probability *p*, the expected loss upon bust (reaching maximum depth) grows as a geometric series in *m*, while the probability of reaching successive depth levels compounds approximately as *p^k*. The critical quantity governing viability is the product *p* x *m*: when *p* x *m* < 1.0, the expected loss contribution from each successive depth level decreases, and the strategy is asymptotically convergent under repeated cycles; when *p* x *m* > 1.0, expected loss diverges with depth and ruin is structurally guaranteed in the limit. The bound follows from classical analysis of doubling strategies (Doob, 1953; Ethier, 2010) and applies directly to grid-hedged execution on liquid spot instruments where each entry incurs spread cost.

The Kelly criterion provides the canonical alternative to Martingale doubling. Kelly (1956), in his foundational paper on information-theoretic interpretation of betting, derived the bet-sizing rule that maximises the long-run logarithmic growth rate of capital under known win probabilities and payoff ratios. Thorp (1969) applied the Kelly criterion to favourable gambling games and contrasted its proportional sizing with the doubling structure of Martingale strategies, showing that proportional sizing is optimal in a precise growth-rate sense while doubling exposes the bettor to ruin in finite time even on positive-expectancy games. The collected papers in MacLean, Thorp and Ziemba (2010) develop modern Kelly capital-growth theory and survey both its strengths (asymptotic growth optimality) and limitations (sensitivity to misestimated probabilities, large drawdowns in transient periods). The relevance to the present work is positioning: Martingale-family strategies are recognised as suboptimal compared to Kelly-style proportional sizing under known win probabilities, yet they remain widely deployed in retail and algorithmic forex because their per-cycle win rate is high and their failure mode is rare but catastrophic. This makes them a stringent test case for adaptive risk management rather than a recommendation as a return-generation method.

The practitioner-oriented literature on cascading-loss position sizing develops the same ideas in trading-specific terms. Vince (1995) treats the mathematics of money management for systems with non-trivial loss-distribution tails, including the geometric-progression sizing that grid-Martingale strategies inherit. Lopez de Prado (2018) provides the modern financial-machine-learning treatment of position sizing, backtest design, and the simulation-to-deployment gap (the latter directly relevant to our real-engine fitness evaluation choice, Section 4). Our adaptive sizer (Section 3.5) combines elements of both lineages: a confidence-conditioned base size (informed by Kelly-type proportional reasoning), a drawdown-conditioned shrinkage factor (informed by Vince's loss-distribution framing), and a per-island evolved exponent that lets the GA tune the aggressiveness of the proportional response per regime.

Martingale-family strategies therefore serve as a stringent test case for regime-aware parameter optimisation. The strategy's sensitivity to market conditions is extreme; the consequences of misconfiguration are severe; and the parameter space (grid spacing, multiplier progression, depth limit, take-profit distance, sizing curve) directly maps to the strategy's risk-reward characteristics. A system that can adapt these parameters to market regime provides meaningful risk reduction in the scenario where it is most needed. Equally important, the strategy's structural difficulty under realistic spread costs (Section 6.1 reports a baseline profit factor of 0.717 with 84.7% maximum drawdown on EUR-USD 5m under OANDA spreads) means that any positive result must be examined for what it actually accomplishes: alpha generation, risk bounding, or both. This research argues for the second interpretation in §7.
